race.name: Amy's Gran Fondo
race.tagline: 103 km along Australia's Great Ocean Road. A ride built on memory — honoring Amy Gillett, the cyclist killed in 2005 whose legacy reshaped cycling safety in Australia.

vitals.location: Lorne, Great Ocean Road, Victoria, Australia
vitals.registration: Online via amysgranfondo.org.au. AUD $100-150 (~$65-100 USD) depending on distance and registration period.
vitals.feed_zones: Feed zones along the Great Ocean Road and through the Otway Ranges. Water, electrolytes, fruit, and energy food. Medical support at key points.
vitals.cutoff_time: Generous cutoff. Full course support and SAG vehicles.

climate.description: September in coastal Victoria is Australian spring — unpredictable and often dramatic. Temperatures of 55-70F (12-21C) with the Southern Ocean pushing weather systems through rapidly. The Great Ocean Road is exposed to coastal wind and weather. Cloud, sun, rain, and wind can cycle through in a single hour. Cool mornings are common. The Otway Ranges can be cooler and wetter than the coast.
challenges: Variable spring weather — sun, rain, wind can change rapidly
Coastal wind exposure along the Great Ocean Road
Otway Ranges can be cooler and wetter than the coastal sections

terrain.primary: Great Ocean Road coastal and Otway Ranges climbing
terrain.surface: Paved roads throughout — the Great Ocean Road is well-maintained. Coastal riding with ocean views, then inland climbs through the Otway Ranges temperate rainforest. Rolling with several sharp ascents. The Great Ocean Road hugs the coast with curves and elevation changes. Otway climbs are forested and atmospheric.
features: Great Ocean Road — one of the world's most scenic coastal roads
Otway Ranges climbs through temperate rainforest

fondo_rating.scoring_notes: Migrated from gravel-race-automation. Original score: 56. adventure dimension dropped, road_surface added. Score recalculated: 56.

course_description.character: Coastal beauty and forested climbing along one of the world's great roads. The route starts in Lorne and rides the Great Ocean Road — Southern Ocean on one side, Otway Ranges forest on the other. The coastal sections are rolling with curves that hug the cliffs. Inland diversions climb through temperate rainforest — tall eucalyptus, tree ferns, dappled light. The route finishes in Aireys Inlet. The terrain is moderate — challenging enough to feel earned, accessible enough that the ride's meaning reaches everyone.
course_description.signature_challenge: The Great Ocean Road itself — not a single climb or feature, but 103 km of coastal beauty and Otway forest riding that serves as a stage for remembrance.

logistics.airport: Melbourne Tullamarine (MEL) — international and domestic connections. Lorne is approximately 2.5 hours southwest. Avalon Airport (AVV) — budget domestic flights, closer to Geelong and the Great Ocean Road.
logistics.lodging_strategy: Lorne is a small coastal town — accommodation is limited and books out fast for event weekend. Book months in advance. Apollo Bay and Aireys Inlet are nearby alternatives. Melbourne accommodation is abundant but means a 2.5-hour drive. Budget AUD $150-250/night in Lorne. Many riders stay in Geelong (1 hour from Lorne) for more options.
logistics.food: Great Ocean Road towns have solid cafe and restaurant scenes — Australian coastal cuisine with good coffee. Lorne's main strip has multiple options. Carb loading: find a pasta or fish-and-chips spot. Post-ride: coffee and a pie from a local bakery. Australian cycling culture runs on flat whites.

history.origin_story: Created in memory of Amy Gillett, an Australian Olympic rower who transitioned to competitive cycling and was killed at age 29 by a motorist while training with the Australian national cycling team on a road in Zeulenroda, Germany, on July 18, 2005. Six of her teammates were also hit — all survived with serious injuries. Her death galvanized Australian cycling safety advocacy. The Amy Gillett Foundation was established to reduce the incidence of cycling fatalities, and the gran fondo became its flagship fundraising event, using the Great Ocean Road as a stage for both remembrance and the message that every cyclist deserves safe passage.
notable_moments: 2005: Amy Gillett killed by a motorist while training in Germany, catalyzing Australian cycling safety movement
Amy Gillett Foundation established, driving 'a metre matters' minimum passing distance legislation
~2011: First Amy's Gran Fondo on the Great Ocean Road
Event has grown to 3,000-4,000 riders, becoming Australia's premier charity cycling event
history.reputation: Australia's memorial ride. Where the Great Ocean Road meets a cause worth riding for.

biased_opinion.summary: Amy's Gran Fondo is not the hardest ride in Australia, the longest, or the most technically demanding. It's something rarer: a ride with genuine meaning. Amy Gillett was an Olympic rower turned cyclist, killed at 29 by a motorist while training in Germany. Her death shook Australian cycling and sparked a movement — the Amy Gillett Foundation has driven 'a metre matters' legislation and cycling safety awareness across the country. The gran fondo rides the Great Ocean Road in her memory, and 3,000-4,000 cyclists show up each year not just to ride, but to ride for something. The course is beautiful — Southern Ocean coastline, Otway rainforest, koalas in eucalyptus trees. But the beauty that matters most is the purpose.
strengths: The cause: riding in memory of Amy Gillett and supporting cycling safety has genuine emotional weight
Great Ocean Road: one of the world's great coastal roads, experienced from the saddle
Community: 3,000-4,000 riders united by a cause deeper than fitness goals
Accessibility: 103 km with moderate climbing welcomes riders of all abilities
weaknesses: Modest physical challenge: 103 km / 1,500m won't test strong cyclists
Weather lottery: Victorian spring on the coast can deliver anything from sunshine to sideways rain
Not a race: if you want competition, the charity-ride format won't deliver that edge
biased_opinion.bottom_line: A ride where the reason you're riding matters more than how fast you finish — and the Great Ocean Road makes sure you never forget where you are.

final_verdict.one_liner: 103 km along the Great Ocean Road in memory of Amy Gillett. Where cycling safety has a heartbeat.
final_verdict.should_you_race: Yes if you want to ride the Great Ocean Road for a cause that matters to every cyclist who shares the road with cars. Yes if you want a beautiful, accessible ride with a passionate community. Skip if you need serious physical challenge or competitive racing.
final_verdict.alternatives: If you want an Australian gran fondo with more climbing: try the 3 Peaks Challenge. If you want Great Ocean Road cycling without the event: ride it yourself any day. If you want a charity cycling event in North America: try the Gran Fondo Whistler.

youtube_data.search_query: Amy's Gran Fondo gran fondo cycling Lorne

videos.title: 2025 UCI Gran Fondo World Championships, Lorne
videos.description:
2025 UCI Gran Fondo World Championships, Lorne, Australia. 50-54 Age group. 

Recorded are the first and the second climb only, not the whole race.  The second climb starts around 55:00 minutes mark.

If you want to follow the video with rider details here's a link to results where you can lookup rider bib numbers

https://www.multisportaustralia.com.au/races/uci-gran-fondo-world-championships-gran-fondo-2025

Early start of the race, 7:35 for our age group. When I arrived almost an hour early to the start to drop off my backpack, the first two lines were already full of people waiting for the race. 

We had some luck with warm temperatures. 50/50 chance for warm or cold front hitting the race course. It could be 6 in the morning with almost freezing at the top of the climb. At least wind chill factor would say it feels like 0 when going downhill. Happened during the week when I was doing recon rides. But as said, warm front hit the area, and it was nice 13 in the morning, 20s in the afternoon, but windy. Forecast showed 36 km/h wind with stronger gusts.

Plan was to stay in the main bunch and conserve energy as much as possible. The first climb was not that hard, I guess no one was too eager to be at the front of the group breaking head wind. Had one scare after ten minutes of climbing when I almost fell off the bike when there was some commotion in the group.  Since pace was not that hard a huge group of probably around 40 riders or more made it to the top of the first climb together. We also caught some other groups of slower riders from age groups which started before us, so the bunch swelled to a considerable size. Descent and middle flat part went by fast and not much happened there. 
Another change of pace happened when we hit the second climb starting in Forrest. Short bursts here and there, and I reckon at the top of the climb the group shrink to only about 15 riders. Descent started ok, but when we first hit the open area wind gusts started throwing me around. A guy in front of me caught a front wheel speed wobble. Took him about hundred meters to stop. When I finally got around him those in front were too far ahead to get back in their slip stream and I really didn’t want to risk it too much with wind still throwing me around like a rag doll.

When I finally made it to the Great Ocean Road I guess I had about 40 seconds behind the first group. I think some of the guys that were dropped on the descent managed to get back to the group. 4 or 5 of them. Missed those, so I rode the last 30 km in a group behind. 

Finished 23rd.

ride on strava:
https://www.strava.com/activities/16185910761
videos.curation_reason: Recent first-person race footage focusing on the key climbs with good view count.

videos.title: UCI GranFondo 2025
videos.channel: SelfRighteousCyclist - Ride it, Write it, Show it
videos.description:
The UCI World Age Group Championships, starting from Lorne, Victoria, Australia.  The course started with a 10km climb at 4.1% and headed out into the hinterland, before returning to the range and up and over to the famous Great Ocean Road with 40km to go. All up about 1850 vertical metres.  
Racing in the Masters 6 (55-59 category) was a great experience to be riding with so many worthy competitors from around the world.  Thanks to those who travelled from far and wide to do the event. I hope the Japanese rider who fell off his bike on the climb is ok.

If you are thinking of doing Amy's Gran Fondo in the future or just looking for a challenging course, this ride will not disappoint.

videos.title: 2025 Amy's Great Ocean Road Gran Fondo Highlights
videos.description:
Thousands of riders took part in Amy’s Great Ocean Road Gran Fondo and the 2025 UCI Gran Fondo World Championships, celebrating a world-class festival of cycling on one of the most breathtaking courses in the world. 

From elite athletes chasing rainbow jerseys to local legends tackling the 122km Recreational Ride, 45km Medio Fondo from Apollo Bay and the Family Fondo — every pedal stroke helped honour Amy Gillett’s legacy and raise funds to make cycling safer across Australia. 💕

We're so excited to announce Amy's Great Ocean Road Gran Fondo will be held on Sunday 13th of September 2026! 

Amy’s 122km Age Category Race is back — and is a qualifying event for the 2027 UCI Gran Fondo World Championships will be held in the French Alps region of Haute‑Savoie, France.

videos.description:

Watch how the race unfolds, and ride the journey with me.

Lastly, please make sure you 'like & subscribe' it does the channel a heap of good and it spreads the word about bike racing!

and 634 Brenton Butaneer. These guys are

And you can see by the definition of

couple of the 19 to 34 age group as well

us. But now it's all about getting some
nutrition on board and waiting to see
what that next move looks like up the
road. Let's go to that. Now,
I don't have much patience at all
because after 76 minutes and maybe 20
minutes of sitting in the bunch, I
decided to light one up. Now, keeping in
mind, there have been a few moves go up
the road, some small attacks. And if I
remember correctly on this one, I think
we just chased back some Polish guys
where I decided to launch a
counterattack with this rider from
Portugal. How awesome is that? Decided
to bridge across with me to roll some
turns. Now you can see in the distance a
couple of more scattered of the 19 to 34
age group riders once again where I'm
always looking to try to motivate those
guys to help me roll through. Now this
is a tricky part of the course because
it is a dead straight road. So the guys
behind us can see exactly where we are
and we give them a nice little carrot to
chase. But you know this is the world
champs. All it takes is a couple of guys
sitting up, no one wanting to do any
work, thinking that the other teams are
going to bring it back for us to get a
nice little gap that we can carry all
the way to the finish. So eventually my
Portugal companion and I eventually
caught up to the 19 to 34 age group as
we catch him on this little riser here.
Now, keeping in mind, we still have
about 20 minutes of riding or about 14
km before we make our way to the start
of that next major climb, which is going
to be extremely decisive part of this
race. And one, you don't want to be
going in already halfcooked. You want to
make sure you're firing all cylinders
when you get there with the right amount
of carbs and the right amount of
caffeine coursing through the body so
you're as fresh as possible. 70 km left
and we are nearing the start of the
first of the two major climbs that's
going to split this race right apart.
Now knowing I've got all this programmed
into my Garmin, so I know exactly what
the climbs are going to be like when we
get there, how long the climbs are going
to be, and where the climb is finished.
So I'm well positioned to know when this
is going to happen. Now, what I want to
do is before we get to the start of that
climb, I'm going to want to move myself
forward into this bike race so that I'm
near the front. Now, I want to be near
the front for two reasons. One is so
that I can drift back through the bunch
if the legs start to fail on me and then
I can snap back onto the last rider.
Essentially, gives me a little bit of a
handicap on the climbs. And the second
reason why I want to be up the front is
I don't want to leave uh opportunities
to another rider to drop a wheel, which
is only going to force my heart rate up
and spike those watts in order to close
that gap. But the problem with this idea
is that there's probably 30 or 40 other
riders with exactly the same idea. And
that's naturally just going to bring the
pace of the ride up and it's going to
make it harder to do that. So doing it
early and then fighting from the front
is a lot easier than having to battle to
the front when the speeds are high. So
here we are. We're about to enter that
climb. And I'm sitting probably too far
back in hindsight. I'm looking uh my
head's kind of peeking up. I'm looking
above the riders to try to find a bit of
an exit strategy where I can move up.
And this is where I do that. Now I go on
the left hand side here in the gutter
trying to make sure that I'm not going
to be riding into any sort of road
debris or anything like that. And here I
am. I've just moved up 15 or so places.
Sitting nice on the inside. I'll have a
bit of a chat to this New Zealand guy.
Let him know that I'm here to not give
me a chop. Some riders are nicer about
that than others. And now I'm in a
really good spot. I can move around in
the bunch. My elbows are out if I need
to move my way through. And I'm keeping
an eye on these Polish riders cuz
they're the ones that have been ultra
aggressive. And I mean that in a nice
way all race. And that's what we love to
see.
With over an hour and 40 minutes of
climbing under the legs, it is time to
hit that first climb, the one out of
forest. Now, it's a pretty steady
gradient of about 4% the entire way,
meaning the draft does play a pretty
major part in this climb. You can see
we're averaging around almost 20 km up
here. That's not the real concern
though, this one. It's the next climb
here. The next climb is one that's
really, really steep and the one I'm
worried about actually out of all this.
But I found myself in a good wheel. I
was just on Pogbach's wheel. No,
obviously not Pog, but I'm on Slovenia's
wheel here, who we'll talk about a
little bit later. Bit of a decisive
character that one. Um, but I'm keeping
near the front. You can see I'm in the
top, say 15 wheels, which is where I
want to be. Uh, keeping those watts as
low as I possibly can be. And you can
see here I'm just drifting back through
the pack, which I'm not in the slightest
concerned about. I'm still keeping near
the front. But the most thing I'm
focused on is keeping that heart rate
low and keeping my watts as low as
possible. Nice and smooth is the aim of
the game here. The legs are in a bit of
pain, but one way to take your mind off
it is having a bit of a look at the
surrounding environment. It's absolutely
beautiful up here in Forest. And I've
actually spent a fair bit of time up
here on the mountain bikes. Some
beautiful trails and gravel trails to be
ridden all around here. But mountain
biking and gravel is far from my mind
right now. We've got another This is one
of the Polish riders that's just come up
on the right hand side and that's
ringing some slight alarm bells for me.
And now we are getting a bit more riders
coming over the top. It does mean the
pace has dropped off quite a bit down to
16 km an hour there. Any slower you're
going to get I'm just going to get shot
right out the back door because it's so
easy for the guys behind to move up and
around. And the last thing I want to be
done is boxed in at this very moment. If
I get boxed in and there's a surge
that's up the road, I'm not going to be
able to make my way out of this mess.
And it means I'm going to have to do
double the amount of effort that I would
rather do if a little move goes up the
road. Now, we're getting closer to the
first major top of this climb. Small
descent straight into the next one. So,
let's skip into that one. Now with 60 km
left, we've had a small bit of rest bite
as we've just come out of that descent
from that first climb of about 4 5 6%
and now it's into the series of next
climbs. So the first one is right in
front of us staring right at it now.
It's a 3minut power effort climb up this
mini ascent of about 1 km at 6%. You can
see I've moved my way right to the front
here. I want to make sure that I'm
sitting right at the bike front of the
bike race here in case any sort of moves
go up the road. From here, it then
continues a drag of about 5 km with an
average of 2.5% with pinches getting up
to 6 to 8%. So, the race doesn't finish
at the top of this little climb. It
continues that drag up. Now, look, the
pace is starting to increase. 16 km an
hour pushing out 400 W. And this is when
the first move goes. We've got Spain
launching off the front with Poland on
the wheel straight away. I found myself
right on the inside on this French
riders wheel here, but he's starting to
fade. You can see his hips start to rock
and roll. And this is the time that I
needed to go. This is where the move is
starting to begin. You can see little
gaps are already starting to form up the
road. I need to get across. That's where
I dig into my reserves. 500 W to get
across to this as I power past this
rider from Spain. in order to get there.
I'm praying right now that he's going to
be able to make it across to here. We're
a bike length away. I think we're good.
And we get across just fine. That is the
first split. Now, the race isn't over.
178 beats. I am on the limit now. There
is no resting up here. There's a nice
little group that's now formed across
the road here with a Polish rider, a
Slovenian rider, a Portugal rider who I
was in the break with before, and a
Spanish rider. And that's when the
Polish rider decides to go again to put
everybody else under a little bit more
pressure. If only we could see what was
going on in the rear cam. I can
guarantee you it was single file and
there was a whole lot of panic stations
and alarm bells going off. Now, that
little spike has forced another split
with the group in front. And this is
that decisive effort where I needed to
get across that wheel now to in order to
be not left in the group behind playing
catchup for the remainder of the race.
So I dig a little bit deeper again to
get across this. I want to close that
gap as soon as I can because we've been
racing for almost 2 hours and 72 km of
hard racing and climbing in the legs.
I'm unsure of where I am at a fitness
level. So I got to make sure that if I
there's a gap, I'm going to close that
straight away. And I think that's where
Poland senses weakness once again and
sends another rider up on the outside to
put everybody under a bit more pressure.
Again, I wish I had the rear cam. I can
guarantee you the riders below is split
to smitherreens. There are riders across
the road. It's a war zone back there.
Riders will be scrambling in order to
get across to this. But lucky for us,
this is that first little bit of rest
bite that we have, that first bit of
descending that we have. But I'm hoping
that people start to freewheel. But we
want to keep that gap extended from the
group behind. We've been able to
establish a small little break. Let's
keep on the pedals. Let's stretch that
out as far as we can. But when my heart
rate's at 178, I'm up there on the limit
now. I'm digging really deep into that
V2 max sort of area. Just wanting to
stay in this race to survive. This is
the decisive move and the last thing I
want to do is pull that rip cord, head
back to the pelaton because my legs were
a little bit sore. You got to dig deep
here and this is one of those times
where I need to do it. So, we had that
very quick little bit of descent and
then it's back straight on that next
little pinch. The climbing is not over
yet. And I'm not too sure if you caught
him in the distance there. That's Mark
O'Brien. We finally caught him. He's
been off the front for 114
minutes. 2 hours he's been out there
just tapping around on his own. It must
have been those S bottles and I had a
cheeky glimpse at his bottle cages
there. I reckon he's had some assistance
from some of the lower grades that might
have thrown him a bit on or maybe he's
picked one up at one of the feed
stations. Nothing wrong with that there.
Marco, let us know how you got on in
that two hours when you're off the
front. Be lovely to know. All right, so
another little attack has gone and it is
Poland that's putting everybody under
pressure here. Portugal flicks the elbow
and now's the time I need to get across.
So I drop in another gear and try to 600
W in order to get across to this. I am
not used to having attacks thrown at me
on descents, but that's how the Euros
like to race and I'm fully on board for
that. So let's look at the makeup here.
In front of me, we've got the Pog from
Slovenia. And then two Polish guys are
in this mix. Behind me, we've got a guy
from Portugal. And there's a couple more
riders that are behind them to make up
this nice little uh group of riders
leading this race.
A couple of minutes later and the chain
has still been tight. There has been
zero let offs, zero free wheels, just
hard watts being poured into this bike
race late in the day. Another move goes
up the road as Poland once again left
hooks, right hooks, throwing it at us in
order to try to split that race apart.
You can see as we come under the gantry
here, notifying us of the climbing being
completed. And I thought, okay, the
climbing's done. Maybe the race will sit
up. Maybe we'll just ride tempo until we
get to the descent. Definitely not.
That's not going to happen. I am on the
absolute limit now. 182 beats. I'm
proper cooked. I'm just praying right
now that this rider can get me across to
the to the main group, which they do.
And I'm staying nice and tiny, nice and
small in the draft in order to get
across. This is the group. Now, you can
see there's been a couple of additions
to the to the makeup of the race. We now
have three, that's right, three Polish
riders in the break of the day. three
riders. What an incredible uh position
for the Polish team to be in. Mixing it
up, we've got team Brazil as well. One
rider from Brazil's been able to make it
across. Um you saw him right at the
start of the race. I gave him a bit of a
hard time for wearing long legs. I'm
sure he's paying the consequences for
that. Now it's about 22° at this stage
of the race. Then we've got team
Slovenia. Look, I'm not going to lie, he
was a pest. He refused to chase moves.
He refused to pull throughs. He chopped
the other riders. He was a pest in this
race. And fair play to him. If that's
how he wants to go about it, then that's
how he's going to go about it. But he
definitely didn't make friends in this
race. He made it hard for everyone.
We're skipping in to 50 km left. We're
just at the flat point about to descend
into Skreek and it's when the two Polish
riders decide to launch another attack
whilst uh whilst everyone's still
fatigued. And I have to spike up to,00 W
in order to shut the door and close this
gap. Oh, I'm going into the red in order
to do that. And the legs are absolutely
screaming at this very moment. But I've
got to make sure I just follow the
moves, stay within the race. I don't
want to let anything get up the road
where I'm caught out of position and
then, you know, out of the race
completely, knowing full well we have a
descent coming up. I just want to be
near the front in case anything happens.
Now, let's talk a little bit about the
wind. Everyone's been going on how it's
going to be hurricane force conditions.
It was going to be chaos. It was going
to be super super hard and windy. We've
been in the rainforest protected from
the wind by the trees and the land. So,
we haven't really noticed too much of
the effects, but we do know it is going
to come. 50 km left in this race. And
the the the little attack that's just
gone up the road has finally settled
down. We're just waiting for that next
counter. Who's going to throw it? I'm
sure another Polish rider is ready and
waiting to go. And if you just wait for
the inside here, that's when Marco,
after being off the front for two hours,
it's time for his repeatability to come
into show. He's got an incredible
ability to continuously hit people with
an attack. Rest for a minute, hit him
again, rest, hit him again. He'll do
this all day. Is he going to be able to
split the group? Is he going to be able
to shake these team Polish riders off
his tail? We'll have to wait and see.
We're finally on to one of the main
descents of this race. Now, normally, if
it's a classic road race, this is when
you get your nutrition in. You kind of
park it up, take it easy, cruise down
through the corners, keeping everything
nice and safe. Not today. Not today at
all. This is the UCI World Grand Fondo
Championships we have down here in lawn
and it is full gas. Poland again
smacking us when everyone sits up with
another attack. And I'm going deep to
get across this 700 watts in order to
close this gap cuz I want to close it
right now. There's two reasons why I'm
jumping straight away across this. One
of those is that I was already carrying
that momentum and it only took me a few
pedal strokes to get across. The other
reason is the Polish guys have two other
teammates in this group. They won't be
chasing this. So, this is a great
opportunity for me to maybe sneak across
with him, bridge the gap, and maybe go
one and two down on the Great Ocean
Road. Who knows? Now, one of the big
issues I'm having is being able to have
a big enough, tall enough gear in order
to close these moves. I find myself
spinning out at some of these stages,
but that's the least of my concerns.
Remember, I spoke about the wind, how
we've been protected. Well, look how
open it gets right here. We've got 50 km
winds ripping from right to left here
and it's forced those riders into the
gutter and they've had a huge crash in
the 19 to34 age group. Did you notice
how much our bikes were twitching
through that part of the course? I had a
really hard time keeping my bike under
control. You can see the leaves of the
trees being blown around all over the
place. This was one of the sketchiest
moments in this race. And the way I
combed that was like what the Polish
guys doing is in the drops and getting
as small as possible on the bike. I'm
trying to bring my center of gravity to
as low as I possibly can and being as
small as I possibly can. So the wind's
going to play a smaller effect on me.
However, we're entering the fastest
descent going round at the moment. This
is where it drops 500 m in altitude in 8
km. That's a 5.5% gradient. Obviously,
there's going to be pinches and flat
spots in there as well. We're entering
the mess and the drop riders of the 18
to 34 group. This is lining up to be
absolute carnage. We were lucky to make
it down that descent. It was one of the
fastest descents I've ever had and for a
moment almost had the cal of the
descent. It was absolutely outrageous
how fast we were taking it down there.
Now, as you see on that sign there,
there's 5 km left in this race. And
unfortunately, I wasn't able to film
everything due to running out of
battery. So, we're going to have to pick
it up from here. Now, a fair bit has
taken place since we last spoke. Almost
as soon as we got onto Great Ocean Road,
the attack started coming thick and
fast. Honestly, it was absolutely
relentless. There was no rest bite at
all. First up, Marco would send one up
the road. Second, a Polish guy would go
again. Then, Marco would send one. The
Polish guy would bridge it back. Marco
would go again and again and again until
finally the rubber band snapped and I
couldn't go on with that anymore. And
then it was me, the only Aussie with
five other internationals. And normally
in a road race, I've raced with a lot of
the Australian guys, so I know what
their attributes are. Are they
sprinters? Are they climbers? Are they
breakaway specialists? I've got no idea
what these guys can do. All I know is
that they're here for a reason and they
are super super strong. So strong that
the Japanese guy going off the front
there, he's in the 18 to 34 group. He
just wants to keep the pace high so they
don't get chased behind us. And that's
fine by us. So, who's left in this race?
We have a Slovenian. We have a
Portuguese guy who I was in the break
with earlier. Uh we have the Brazilian
guy with the long legs. a Polish guy and
to finish things off, a guy from
Ecuador. Now, keep in mind, there are
three guys up the road at the moment.
There's Marco, and of course, you
guessed it, two other Polish guys have
been able to go with him. So,
essentially, we're no longer even racing
for a medal. We're racing for a top 10.
So, you could finish anywhere from
fourth all the way to ninth in this
little group. And I wanted to make sure
that I finished fourth. But you always
hold out just the slightest bit of hope,
the slightest bit of hope that those
three riders that have gone up the road
start foxing early. And if we keep the
pace just high enough, there's a chance,
a small chance, but there's still a
chance they're able to chase them back
and sprint for first, second, or third.
But we're definitely running out of
time. We're coming into 3 km to go here.
Oh, it's going to be very, very close to
the finish. So, with 2 and 1/2ks to go,
everyone's starting to get a little bit
towy and a little bit unsure about how
they're going to win this race. If
you're not a sprinter, you've got to
take advantage of these spicy little
parts of the race where the legs are
dead and the road is steep to really put
everyone under a little bit of pressure.
And that's exactly what Patrick from
Slovenia does. He sends a nice little
attack up the road. And I have to go
here. This is life or death essentially.
So I spike up to 700 W and I see the
Brazilian rider struggling to close. So
I'm not taking any chances. So I make
sure I super glue myself to the
Equatoran rider here who's sitting nice
and comfortably on Patrick from
Slovenia's wheel and then it's time for
a bit of a rest. 176 beats. We've gone
deep for that one. And you just saw
we've just passed the 2 km barrier. 2ks
left in the 2025 UCI Grand Fondo race
and we could finish anywhere from 4th to
9th. And after almost 3 and 1/2 hours of
racing and climbing, we are coming in to
1 kilometer left in the 2025 UCI Grand
Fondo Road Race. And these riders are
all still together. We can still finish
from fourth to 9inth and we still
haven't been able to catch a glimpse of
the riders up the road. Now, I have zero
idea of any of these guys to sprint. So,
I've got to make sure I'm marking
everybody, keeping it in a gutter, and
making sure that nobody rolls me at the
finish line. I have actually never seen
the finish line ever since Amy's. So,
I've really no idea how this is going to
turn out. All I know as soon as we get
into the barriers, that's essentially
going to be 200 m to go. And that's
where essentially I'm going to look to
open up my sprint. So, here we go.
Coming into 800 m. I'm third wheel. And
I'm really happy with this. I don't want
to overlap my wheel with the Polish guy
here. I want to make sure I've got a gap
where I can jump out and have open space
to the finish. However, I'm concerned
now that this Ecuadorian is taking my
exit stratey, taking my line away from
me. So, I don't know where I can go. I'm
trying to find a little exit. I'm going
right. I'm going left. I'm trying to
keep those options open. Coming into 500
m. And we're looking around. We're
trying to get an idea of where the other
rid is here. Who is the first person to
jump? I'm not jumping till about 300 to
go. And there you saw 300 m to go. And
that's when the Ecuadorian lights up his
sprint, but it's slow. I've got these
guys covered. 200 m left. My head goes
down and I give it all the watts I
possibly can. I have a cheeky glimpse
over my shoulder. I'm in the clear. 100
m left. 50 m to go. Another glimpse. I
have a look. I have daylight behind me.
I celebrate across the line. I have just
finished fourth in the 2025 UCI Grand
Fondo Road Race and I cannot believe it.
The cycling aside, I think what was so
great about this event is racing with
internationals. You know, I met a
Lebanese guy uh in the TT and I had a
chat to him about how Lebanon was that I
traveled through there and it was a
beautiful country with most delicious
apples you'll ever have. There was a
Slovenian guy who was a bit of a fogg,
but after the race he was a lovely guy.
And then there was the Poles who was
super super friendly, super strong,
raced aggressively. And that's the sort
of thing that we love and makes cycling
so great. It brings all these fantastic
people together to share one common
interest that we can smash ourselves to
pieces and at the end of the day have a
beer at the pub, which I know a few of
the boys did. Unlike me who had to make
the drive back to Melbourne, I was
absolutely cooked. I had to uh pump the
air con and crank the music in order to
stay awake. It was a grim ride home. But
ladies and gentlemen, that is a wrap.
That's a wrap. Thank you so much for
joining me on this journey. I really do
hope you had a great time. If you do
like the racing and that sort of thing,
please make sure you do hit that
subscribe button as it helps the channel
grow and allows me to bring more races
to you guys. Uh if you see yourself,
make sure you chuck a comment below. I'd
love to hear from you. Let me know how
your race went. And that's a wrap. Well,
I'm going to make like a cockatu and I
don't know, leave. Thanks for watching.
Ciao.
videos.curation_reason: First-person race recap from recent UCI Gran Fondo with excellent view count and detailed race experience.

quotes.text: 131ks of pain. Starts off by a 20 minute threshold effort. Bit of reprieve, bit of downhill, and then the final decisive climb in forest. From there, after the crazy descent, you're making way to a good old 30ish km smash back along the Great Ocean Road.
Amy's ride. It's just the community, the great ocean road, the environment as well. Just love it. Just to from a tourism point of view to see people from overseas down here is absolutely magic.

key_challenges.name: 20 minute threshold effort at start
key_challenges.description: Starts off by a 20 minute threshold effort. Bit of reprieve, bit of downhill, and then the final decisive climb in forest.

key_challenges.name: Final decisive climb in forest
key_challenges.description: The final decisive climb in forest. From there, after the crazy descent, you're making way to a good old 30ish km smash back along the Great Ocean Road.

terrain_notes.text: 131ks of pain. Starts off by a 20 minute threshold effort. Bit of reprieve, bit of downhill, and then the final decisive climb in forest. From there, after the crazy descent, you're making way to a good old 30ish km smash back along the Great Ocean Road.

gear_mentions.text: Giant Propel SL0 coming in at just under 7 kilos. Fast sports bars, fast sports wheels. Shramm red.

race_day_tips.text: I had two Sull 750 mil bottles filled with 130 g each. And the strategy was keep it simple and keep drinking and eating throughout the race. The last thing I wanted to do was go hunger flat. So, making sure I was on the gels and smashing those bottles was super super important.

Early start because we needed to be on the start line by about 7. So, that gave us a little bit of time to smash some breakfast. So, I had a whole bunch of oats with some banana and honey, plenty of coffees as well to go around.

additional_quotes.text: We needed to be on the start line by about 7. So, that gave us a little bit of time to smash some breakfast. So, I had a whole bunch of oats with some banana and honey, plenty of coffees as well to go around.
It's really heartwarming and and uplifting. I get pretty emotional and a little bit tearary about it, but that's part of my journey.

rider_intel.search_text: Amy's Gran Fondo in Lorne, Australia features 131 kilometers of challenging terrain along the Great Ocean Road. Riders describe the course as starting with a brutal 20-minute threshold effort, followed by some downhill reprieve before tackling the final decisive climb in forest. After a crazy descent, participants face a demanding 30-kilometer stretch back along the Great Ocean Road. The race attracts around 2,000 riders and creates a vibrant community atmosphere in the heart of Lorne. Successful riders emphasize the importance of consistent nutrition, recommending two 750ml bottles with 130g carbs each and regular gel consumption to avoid bonking. The early 7 AM start time requires careful race morning logistics. The event combines competitive cycling with a meaningful cause, creating an emotional and uplifting experience for participants and spectators alike in one of the world's most scenic coastal locations.
